items.title: Make the underlying vector space explicit
items.avoid: Reasoning about arrays of numbers without specifying what the components mean or which space and basis they live in.
items.prefer: State the underlying space (for example, R^n or a function space) and the chosen basis when interpreting coordinates or comparing results.
items.why: Clarity about the space and basis prevents misinterpretation of coordinates and makes it easier to reason about transformations and projections.
items.example: When explaining PCA results, say "these coordinates are in the basis of the top principal components" instead of only listing numeric vectors.

items.title: Verify subspace criteria instead of relying on plots
items.avoid: Assuming a set of states or vectors is a subspace because it looks like a line or plane in a scatter plot.
items.prefer: Check membership of the zero vector and closure under addition and scalar multiplication before applying subspace reasoning.
items.why: Plots can be misleading and do not reveal behavior under scaling or addition; using subspace tools on non-subspaces invalidates arguments.
items.example: The set of probability vectors in R^n with non-negative entries that sum to 1 is convex but not a subspace, so arbitrary linear combinations will leave the simplex.

items.title: Track dimension and independence when adding features
items.avoid: Blindly adding new features or embedding dimensions without checking whether they add genuinely new directions.
items.prefer: Use rank and independence checks to detect redundant vectors and keep only features that meaningfully increase the span.
items.why: Redundant features waste capacity, can hurt conditioning and may overcomplicate model explanations without adding expressive power.
items.example: If a new feature is almost a linear combination of existing ones (for example, total_spend = price * quantity), treat it carefully or encode it explicitly as such.
